[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.

Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.

{{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Archaeological sites in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Kofun period sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Hamanaku history]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Ritual sites in Japan]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:11]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': * Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': * Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

{{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Archaeological sites in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Kofun period sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Hamanaku history]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Ritual sites in Japan]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

{{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Archaeological sites in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Kofun period sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Hamanaku history]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Ritual sites in Japan]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:enpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라}}

{{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Archaeological sites in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Kofun period sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Hamanaku history]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Ritual sites in Japan]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:enpaku Iwakura Site]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.

It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.

The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by burial mounds such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라}}

The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}

In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
