[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|12=draft}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚|12=draft}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=draft}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|12=draft}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート|12=draft}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]
{{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=Tenpaku Iwakura Site}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tenpaku Iwaza Ruins]] [[Category:Archaeological sites in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun period sites]] [[Category:Hamanaku history]] [[Category:Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:Ritual sites in Japan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] }} [[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[sn:enpaku Iwakura Site]] [[en:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区|12=draft}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=draft}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|12=draft}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚|12=draft}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=draft}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|12=draft}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート|12=draft}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=50|lat_sec=0.22|lon_deg=137|lon_min=39|lon_sec=48.68|label=天白磐座
遺跡|position=top|width=220|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|天白磐座遺跡の位置}}}} '''天白磐座遺跡'''（てんぱくいわくらいせき）は、[[静岡県]][[浜松市]][[浜名区]]引佐町（いなさちょう）[[井伊谷]]（いいのや）にある[[古代]][[祭祀]][[遺跡]]。静岡県指定史跡に指定されている（指定名称は「渭伊神社境内遺跡」）。 == 概要 == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[渭伊神社]]}}{{small|社殿背後の山上に天白磐座遺跡が所在する。}}]] 静岡県西部、神宮寺川と井伊谷川により形成される小盆地（[[井伊谷]]）の北西部、[[渭伊神社]]（[[延喜式内社]]）後背の薬師山（円錐形、標高41.75メートル）山頂において、巨岩群を神の依代（[[磐座]]）とした古代祭祀遺跡である{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）に祭祀遺跡と確認され、[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）に発掘調査が実施されている{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 磐座は西・東・北の巨岩3つを主体として構成された大規模なものになる。特に西岩の西壁直下では、[[古墳時代]]の手づくね土器200以上・滑石製勾玉・鉄矛・鉄刀・鉄鏃・鉇など、古墳時代前期後葉から[[平安時代]]中期の祭祀遺物が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また西岩・東岩の間では、続く平安時代後半以降の[[経塚]]関連遺物が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 この天白磐座遺跡については、神宮寺川が井伊谷に流入する喉元にあたる位置にあることから、水源（水分）祭祀の性格が指摘される{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また祭祀の主体としては、井伊谷の東方丘陵上の首長墓系譜（[[北岡大塚古墳]]→馬場平古墳→馬場平3号墳→谷津古墳）が想定される{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。なお、現在は渭伊神社（通称八幡宮）の境内に位置するが、これについて渭伊神社は磐座祭祀の延長として創祀されたとする説（八幡宮は渭伊神社と別神社で[[16世紀]]前半頃に[[龍潭寺]]付近から遷座合祀）がある一方{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}史跡説明板。、渭伊神社は磐座祭祀と連続しないとする説（八幡宮と渭伊神社は同一神社で16世紀前半頃に龍潭寺付近から遷座）がある{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 遺跡域は[[1992年]]（[[平成]]4年）に「渭伊神社境内遺跡」として静岡県指定史跡に指定されている。 == 遺跡歴 == * [[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）1月、引佐町内の遺跡分布調査において、手づくね土器の散布により祭祀遺跡と確認{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 * [[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）8月、発掘調査（[[同志社大学]]、[[1992年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 * 1992年（[[平成]]4年）3月17日、「渭伊神社境内遺跡」として静岡県指定史跡に指定。 == 遺構 == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|西岩（岩A） 西壁}}{{small|壁直下では、発掘調査において古墳時代の祭祀遺物が出土している。}}]] 磐座は[[チャート]]の露頭・転石であり{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}、西・東・北の巨岩3つを主体として構成される。特に西の巨岩（岩A）が大きく、南北10.3メートル・東西6.8メートル・高さ7.4メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また東岩（岩B）は、西岩の東に約4メートルの間隔を空けて相対しており、北東-南西方向が長い矩形で、長軸約7メートル・短軸4.1メートル・高さ5.2メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。北岩（岩C）は、西岩と東岩の中間やや北寄りに位置し、三角形で、底辺3.5メートル前後・高さ2.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 祭祀遺物集中地点の1つは、西岩（岩A）の西壁直下付近（I区・II区）である。西岩の西壁は幅約7メートル・高さ約5.5メートルで垂直に切り立ち、庇状に張り出して半岩陰状をなす。その直下付近で[[古墳時代]]前期後半-後期前半頃の手づくね土器200以上・滑石製勾玉1・鉄矛1・鉄刀1・鉄鏃4・鉇1のほか、[[奈良時代]]の土馬1・[[須恵器]]、[[平安時代]]前半の[[9世紀]]-[[10世紀]]前半頃の[[灰釉陶器]]・須恵器が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。特に鉄矛は、古墳時代の祭祀遺跡からの出土は限定される点で注目される{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。ただし、祭祀期間に比して遺物が極めて少数であることから、祭祀終了後に大半の遺物は別の場所（一説に鳴岩付近）に廃棄された可能性が指摘される{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 祭祀遺物集中地点のもう1つは、西岩・東岩の間の巨岩群中央部である（III区）。ここでは、[[平安時代]]後半以降の[[11世紀]]末葉-[[13世紀]]中葉頃の[[経塚]]関連遺物（経筒外容器6以上・和鏡・[[山茶碗]]）が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。[[末法思想]]のもと経塚が霊地で営まれる例は数多く知られ、当地が霊地として認識されていたことを物語る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 なお、経塚以降の祭祀遺物は認められていない{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, higashi-iwa.jpg|東岩（岩B） ファイル:天白磐座遺跡出土 祭祀遺物.JPG|古墳時代祭祀遺物
{{small|浜松市地域遺産センター展示。}} ファイル:天白磐座遺跡出土 経塚関連遺物.JPG|経塚関連遺物
{{small|浜松市地域遺産センター展示。}} == 文化財 == === 静岡県指定文化財 === * 史跡 ** 渭伊神社境内遺跡 - 1992年（平成4年）3月17日指定[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html 渭伊神社境内遺跡]（浜松市ホームページ）。。 == 関連施設 == * 浜松市地域遺産センター（浜松市北区引佐町井伊谷） - 天白磐座遺跡の出土品等を保管・展示。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 史跡説明板（浜松市設置） * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=辰巳和弘|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=天白磐座遺跡（引佐町の古墳文化V）|publisher=引佐町教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=天白磐座遺跡|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 22 静岡県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=辰巳和弘|year=2006|chapter=|title=聖なる水の祀りと古代王権 天白磐座遺跡（シリーズ「遺跡を学ぶ」33）|publisher=新泉社|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[磐座]] * [[渭伊神社]] * [[おんな城主 直虎]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html 渭伊神社境内遺跡] - 浜松市ホームページ {{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=天白磐座遺跡}} {{デフォルトソート:てんはくいわくらいせき}} [[Category:静岡県の考古遺跡]] [[Category:古墳時代の遺跡]] [[Category:浜名区の歴史]] [[Category:静岡県指定史跡]] [[Category:日本の祭祀遺跡]]

遺跡|position=top|width=220|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|天白磐座遺跡の位置}}}} haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[渭伊神社]]}}{{small|社殿背後の山上に天白磐座遺跡が所在する。}}]] 静岡県西部、神宮寺川と井伊谷川により形成される小盆地（[[井伊谷]]）の北西部、[[渭伊神社]]（[[延喜式内社]]）後背の薬師山（円錐形、標高41.75メートル）山頂において、巨岩群を神の依代（[[磐座]]）とした古代祭祀遺跡である{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）に祭祀遺跡と確認され、[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）に発掘調査が実施されている{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 磐座は西・東・北の巨岩3つを主体として構成された大規模なものになる。特に西岩の西壁直下では、[[古墳時代]]の手づくね土器200以上・滑石製勾玉・鉄矛・鉄刀・鉄鏃・鉇など、古墳時代前期後葉から[[平安時代]]中期の祭祀遺物が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また西岩・東岩の間では、続く平安時代後半以降の[[経塚]]関連遺物が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 この天白磐座遺跡については、神宮寺川が井伊谷に流入する喉元にあたる位置にあることから、水源（水分）祭祀の性格が指摘される{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また祭祀の主体としては、井伊谷の東方丘陵上の首長墓系譜（[[北岡大塚古墳]]→馬場平古墳→馬場平3号墳→谷津古墳）が想定される{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。なお、現在は渭伊神社（通称八幡宮）の境内に位置するが、これについて渭伊神社は磐座祭祀の延長として創祀されたとする説（八幡宮は渭伊神社と別神社で[[16世紀]]前半頃に[[龍潭寺]]付近から遷座合祀）がある一方{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}史跡説明板。、渭伊神社は磐座祭祀と連続しないとする説（八幡宮と渭伊神社は同一神社で16世紀前半頃に龍潭寺付近から遷座）がある{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 遺跡域は[[1992年]]（[[平成]]4年）に「渭伊神社境内遺跡」として静岡県指定史跡に指定されている。 == 遺跡歴 == * [[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）1月、引佐町内の遺跡分布調査において、手づくね土器の散布により祭祀遺跡と確認{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 * [[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）8月、発掘調査（[[同志社大学]]、[[1992年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。 * 1992年（[[平成]]4年）3月17日、「渭伊神社境内遺跡」として静岡県指定史跡に指定。 == 遺構 == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|西岩（岩A） 西壁}}{{small|壁直下では、発掘調査において古墳時代の祭祀遺物が出土している。}}]] 磐座は[[チャート]]の露頭・転石であり{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}、西・東・北の巨岩3つを主体として構成される。特に西の巨岩（岩A）が大きく、南北10.3メートル・東西6.8メートル・高さ7.4メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。また東岩（岩B）は、西岩の東に約4メートルの間隔を空けて相対しており、北東-南西方向が長い矩形で、長軸約7メートル・短軸4.1メートル・高さ5.2メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。北岩（岩C）は、西岩と東岩の中間やや北寄りに位置し、三角形で、底辺3.5メートル前後・高さ2.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 祭祀遺物集中地点の1つは、西岩（岩A）の西壁直下付近（I区・II区）である。西岩の西壁は幅約7メートル・高さ約5.5メートルで垂直に切り立ち、庇状に張り出して半岩陰状をなす。その直下付近で[[古墳時代]]前期後半-後期前半頃の手づくね土器200以上・滑石製勾玉1・鉄矛1・鉄刀1・鉄鏃4・鉇1のほか、[[奈良時代]]の土馬1・[[須恵器]]、[[平安時代]]前半の[[9世紀]]-[[10世紀]]前半頃の[[灰釉陶器]]・須恵器が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。特に鉄矛は、古墳時代の祭祀遺跡からの出土は限定される点で注目される{{Sfn|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}。ただし、祭祀期間に比して遺物が極めて少数であることから、祭祀終了後に大半の遺物は別の場所（一説に鳴岩付近）に廃棄された可能性が指摘される{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 祭祀遺物集中地点のもう1つは、西岩・東岩の間の巨岩群中央部である（III区）。ここでは、[[平安時代]]後半以降の[[11世紀]]末葉-[[13世紀]]中葉頃の[[経塚]]関連遺物（経筒外容器6以上・和鏡・[[山茶碗]]）が検出されている{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。[[末法思想]]のもと経塚が霊地で営まれる例は数多く知られ、当地が霊地として認識されていたことを物語る{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 なお、経塚以降の祭祀遺物は認められていない{{Sfn|辰巳和弘|2006}}。 File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, higashi-iwa.jpg|東岩（岩B） ファイル:天白磐座遺跡出土 祭祀遺物.JPG|古墳時代祭祀遺物
{{small|浜松市地域遺産センター展示。}} ファイル:天白磐座遺跡出土 経塚関連遺物.JPG|経塚関連遺物
{{small|浜松市地域遺産センター展示。}} == 文化財 == === 静岡県指定文化財 === * 史跡 ** 渭伊神社境内遺跡 - 1992年（平成4年）3月17日指定[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html 渭伊神社境内遺跡]（浜松市ホームページ）。。 == 関連施設 == * 浜松市地域遺産センター（浜松市北区引佐町井伊谷） - 天白磐座遺跡の出土品等を保管・展示。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 史跡説明板（浜松市設置） * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=辰巳和弘|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=天白磐座遺跡（引佐町の古墳文化V）|publisher=引佐町教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=天白磐座遺跡|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 22 静岡県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=辰巳和弘|year=2006|chapter=|title=聖なる水の祀りと古代王権 天白磐座遺跡（シリーズ「遺跡を学ぶ」33）|publisher=新泉社|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[磐座]] * [[渭伊神社]] * [[おんな城主 直虎]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html 渭伊神社境内遺跡] - 浜松市ホームページ {{Coord|34|50|0.22|N|137|39|48.68|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=title|name=天白磐座遺跡}} {{デフォルトソート:てんはくいわくらいせき}} [[Category:静岡県の考古遺跡]] [[Category:古墳時代の遺跡]] [[Category:浜名区の歴史]] [[Category:静岡県指定史跡]] [[Category:日本の祭祀遺跡]]

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|ja|浜松市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hamanaku Ward|ja|浜名区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Naotora: The Lady Warlord|ja|おんな城主 直虎|en|6=ko|7=여자 성주 나오토라|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座|lt=Iwakura rocks|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|qq=}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|sutra mound|lt=sutra mounds|ja|経塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sutra mound|qq=}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshisha University|qq=}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|chert|ja|チャート|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chert|qq=}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:浜名区の歴史
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|qq=}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

[[sn:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]] [[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] [[sn:enpaku Iwakura Site]] [[en:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]] [[Category:古墳時代の遺跡]] [[Category:日本の祭祀遺跡]] [[Category:浜名区の歴史]] [[Category:静岡県の考古遺跡]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Shizuoka Prefecture]]

revision.comment: Bot: update category link → Kofun archaeological sites
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|qq=}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.

revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Kofun period sites
revision.text:
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|qq=}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
