revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|qq=|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

[[Category:Kofun archaeological sites]] [[Category:Ritual Archaeological Sites in Japan]] [[Category:History of Hamana Ward]]
[[Category:Designated historic sites of Shizuoka Prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} {{wikidata link|Q87853306}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").

== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

[[Category:Designated historic sites of Shizuoka Prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q87853306}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

{{reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q87853306]] Wikidata name: Tenpaku Iwakura Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in [[:en:Hamamatsu|Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|Hamanaku Ward]], Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including [[:en:Sutra mound|sutra mounds]], were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated [[:en:Chart (disambiguation)|chert]] rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Information Board (Installed by Hamamatsu City) * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|authorlink=辰巳和弘|year=1992|chapter=|title=Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Ancient Culture of Inasa V)|publisher=Inasa Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Tenpaku Iwakura Site|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 22: Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|天白磐座遺跡（平凡社）|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kazuhiko Tatsumi|year=2006|chapter=|title=Sacred Water Rituals and Ancient Authority: Tenpaku Iwakura Site (Learn from Archaeological Sites Series 33)|publisher=Shinsensha|isbn=4787707337|ref={{Harvid|辰巳和弘|2006}}}} == See Also == * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]] * {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Naotora: The Lady Warlord|Naotora: The Lady Warlord]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenpaku Iwakura Site}} * [https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Hamamatsu City Official Website]

== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura rocks}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|Sutra mound|en|Sutra mound|WD=Q7650105|lt=sutra mounds}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|Chart (disambiguation)|en|Chart (disambiguation)|WD=Q1067738|lt=chert}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|Sutra mound|en|Sutra mound|WD=Q7650105|lt=sutra mounds}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|Chart (disambiguation)|en|Chart (disambiguation)|WD=Q1067738|lt=chert}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|en|Hamamatsu|WD=Q185125|lt=Hamamatsu|1=Hamamatsu}}, {{ill|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|en|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|WD=Q117077169|lt=Hamanaku Ward|1=Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.

Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|Chart (disambiguation)|en|Chart (disambiguation)|WD=Q1067738|lt=chert|1=Chart (disambiguation)}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tenpaku Iwakura Site]][[ja:天白磐座遺跡]]
[[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, zentai-1.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|天白磐座遺跡 概観}}{{small|左に西岩（岩A）、右に東岩（岩B）、中央奥に北岩（岩C）。}}]] '''Tenpaku Iwakura Site''' (天白磐座遺跡) is an ancient {{ill|ritual site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located in {{ill|Hamamatsu|en|Hamamatsu|WD=Q185125|lt=Hamamatsu|1=Hamamatsu}}, {{ill|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|en|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|WD=Q117077169|lt=Hamanaku Ward|1=Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu}}, Inasa-chō, Ii-no-ya, Shizuoka Prefecture. It has been designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site (official designation: "Ii Shrine Precinct Site").
It is a place of the {{ill|Tenpaku faith|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku faith|qq=}}.
== Overview == [[File:Ii-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}}}{{small|The Tenpaku Iwakura Site is located on the mountain behind the shrine.}}]] Located in western Shizuoka Prefecture, the site is situated atop Yakushiyama, a conical hill with an elevation of 41.75 meters, behind {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}}, an {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}-listed shrine, in the small basin of Iinoya formed by the Jingūji and Iinoya Rivers. The site features a cluster of massive rocks that served as {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura rocks|1=Iwakura rock}} (Iwakura), believed to be the dwelling place of deities. The site was confirmed as a ritual site in 1988, and excavation was conducted in 1989.
The Iwakura consists of three large rocks: the western rock (A), eastern rock (B), and northern rock (C). Below the western rock’s western wall, numerous ritual artifacts, including over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, talc magatama, iron weapons, and tools, as well as artifacts from the late Kofun to mid-Heian period, were unearthed. Between the western and eastern rocks, relics associated with later ritual practices, including {{ill|Sutra mound|en|Sutra mound|WD=Q7650105|lt=sutra mounds|1=Sutra mound}}, were discovered, indicating continuous ritual significance through the mid-Heian period.
The site's location at the junction of two rivers suggests a connection to water-source rituals. The primary participants in these rituals are believed to be associated with the lineage of local rulers, evidenced by Kofuns such as {{ill|Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|北岡大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaoka Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} and other nearby tombs. Some theories propose that {{ill|Ii Shrine|ja|渭伊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Shrine|qq=}} originated as an extension of Iwakura rituals, though this remains debated.
In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.

The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
== References ==

In 1992, the site was designated as the "Ii Shrine Precinct Site," a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Archaeological History == * 1988: Identified as a ritual site through the distribution of hand-formed pottery during a local survey. * 1989: Excavation conducted by {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}}. * 1992: Designated as a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site.
== Features == [[File:Tenpaku Iwakura Site, nishi-iwa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Western Rock (A) Western Wall}}{{small|Excavations here revealed numerous Kofun-period ritual artifacts.}}]] The site consists of exposed and relocated {{ill|Chart (disambiguation)|en|Chart (disambiguation)|WD=Q1067738|lt=chert|1=Chart (disambiguation)}} rocks forming the sacred area. The primary rocks are: * '''Western Rock (A)''': The largest, measuring 10.3 meters north-south, 6.8 meters east-west, and 7.4 meters tall. * '''Eastern Rock (B)''': Rectangular, located 4 meters east of Rock A, measuring approximately 7 meters along its long axis, 4.1 meters along its short axis, and 5.2 meters in height. * '''Northern Rock (C)''': Triangular, situated slightly north of the central area, with a base of approximately 3.5 meters and a height of 2.7 meters.
Artifacts from ritual activities were concentrated in two locations: 1. Beneath the western wall of Rock A: Excavations revealed over 200 hand-formed pottery pieces, talc magatama, iron weapons, and Heian-period ceramics. 2. Between Rocks A and B: Relics such as sutra containers, mirrors, and earthenware were found, dating from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period.
== Cultural Properties == === Shizuoka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''': ** Ii Shrine Precinct Site - Designated on March 17, 1992<ref name="浜松市">[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/inasa/inasa/iijinjya.html Ii Shrine Precinct Site] (Hamamatsu City official website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Hamamatsu City Regional Heritage Center''' (Hamamatsu City, Kita-ku, Inasa-chō, Iinoya) - Preserves and displays artifacts from the Tenpaku Iwakura Site.
